'''Iestyn Rhys Harris''' (born 25 June 1976) is a former [[List of dual-code rugby internationals|dual-code international]] professional [[rugby league]] and [[rugby union]] footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s, and [[Coach (sport)|coach]] in rugby league in the 2000s and 2010s. He played representative rugby league for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] and [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]], and at club level for the [[Warrington Wolves]], the [[Leeds Rhinos]] ([[Captain (sports)|captain]]) where he won the 1998 [[Man of Steel Awards|Man of Steel Award]], the [[Bradford Bulls]] ([[Captain (sports)|captain]]), and [[Featherstone Rovers]], and representative rugby union for [[Wales national rugby union team|Wales]], and at club level for [[Cardiff RFC]] and [[Cardiff Blues]], and has coached representative rugby league for [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]], and at club level for [[Featherstone Rovers]] (assistant coach), the [[Crusaders Rugby League]] (initially as assistant coach under [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]], and then head coach), the [[Wigan Warriors]] (assistant coach), and [[Salford Red Devils]] (head coach).

==Early life==
Harris was born in [[Oldham]], [[Greater Manchester]], England. His [[Abercarn]]-born grandfather [[Norman Harris (rugby)|Norman Harris]] played rugby union for [[Abercarn RFC]], [[Ebbw Vale RFC]], [[Pontypool RFC]] and [[Newbridge RFC]] before heading north to win eight Wales caps in rugby league.<ref>[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/wales/1643056.stm Iestyn: Angel of the north]</ref>

==Early Rugby League career==
=== Warrington ===
Harris' professional career began with league club [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] at age 17 in 1993<ref>{{cite news |last1=Kilgallon |first1=Steve |title=Iestyn Harris stuns rugby by joining homely Warrington (not) |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2004/apr/01/rugbyleague.rugbyunion |access-date=23 October 2020 |work=The Guardian |date=1 April 2004}}</ref> before a record-breaking £350,000 transfer to [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in 1997.

Iestyn Harris played at {{rlp|ce}} in Warrington's 10–40 defeat by [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1994–95 League Cup (rugby league)|1994–95 Regal Trophy]] Final during the [[1994–95 Rugby Football League season|1994–95 season]] at [[John Smith's Stadium|Alfred McAlpine Stadium]], [[Huddersfield]] on Saturday 28 January 1995.<ref name="28th January 1995: Warrington 10 Wigan 40 (Regal Trophy Final)">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2931|title=28th January 1995: Warrington 10 Wigan 40 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=14 July 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140714151036/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2931|url-status=dead}}</ref>

===Leeds===
Harris was made captain of Leeds Rhinos in his first full season at the club at the age of twenty-one, and became the first Leeds [[Captain (sports)|captain]] in a decade to lift silverware when Leeds Rhinos claimed the 1999 Challenge Cup.

Harris initially played at {{rlp|fb}} before being switched to {{rlp|so}}. In 1998, despite having played most of his games at fullback, he was named in the [[rugby league positions#half-backs|half backs]] in 1998's [[Super League Dream Team]]<ref>{{cite news
|last = Hadfield
|first = Dave
|title = League proposes show in South Africa
|work = [[The Independent]]
|publisher = independent.co.uk
|date = 23 September 1998
|url = https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-league-proposes-show-in-s-africa-1200080.html
|access-date = 24 April 2010
|location=London}}</ref> and also collected the annual [[Man of Steel Awards|Man of Steel]] trophy as the best player in the [[Super League]]. He captained Leeds Rhinos from {{rlp|fb}} in their [[1998 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Wigan Warriors]].

A superb goal-kicker, Harris broke the club record for goals in a season in 1999 with 168, he was named at stand-off in 1999's [[Super League IV]] [[Super League Dream Team|Dream Team]], and broke the 2,000 career points total two years later.

Harris matched the record for scoring the most points in a [[Challenge Cup]] Final when he scored 20 points (1 [[Try (rugby)|try]], and 8 [[Try (rugby)|conversion]]s) in [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]]' 52–16 victory over [[London Broncos]] in the [[1999 Challenge Cup]] Final during [[Super League IV]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on 1 May 1999. This equalled the record set by [[Neil Fox (rugby league)|Neil Fox]] (2 tries and 7 goals) in [[Wakefield Trinity]]'s victory over [[Hull F.C.]] in the 1960 Challenge Cup Final.

==Cardiff and Wales RU==
In 2001, Harris made a £1.5 million switch to [[Cardiff RFC]] and [[Wales national rugby union team|Wales]].

Harris made his debut for the Welsh side on 10 November 2001, playing against [[Argentina national rugby union team|Argentina]] in Cardiff; Wales lost 16–30. Having played only 200 minutes of rugby union before his international debut, Harris struggled, particularly with his tactical kicking. This led to a move from [[fly-half]] to [[Centre (rugby union)|inside centre]], where he could be creative with less pressure.

===World Cup 2003===
Harris played in the [[2003 Rugby World Cup]] in Australia, where Wales lost in the quarter-finals to eventual winners, [[England national rugby union team|England]], despite leading the game for 60 minutes. Harris won 25 Wales caps as a union player but made it clear that he wanted to return to League.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/welsh/3781589.stm A dream unfulfilled]</ref>

==Return to Rugby League==

===Bradford Bulls===
In 2004, Harris returned to rugby league, citing "family reasons". He joined [[Bradford Bulls]] after a contractual fight with former club Leeds. His salary was reputed to be £1 million over four seasons.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/3895371.stm Harris defends Bulls choice]</ref> Harris played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] at {{rlp|so}} in their [[2004 Super League Grand Final]] loss against the [[Leeds Rhinos]]. Harris was selected in the [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain team]] to compete in the end of season [[2004 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] tournament. In the final against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] he played at {{rlp|so}} in the Lions' 44–4 loss.

Harris wrote a book, published in October 2005, detailing his moves between codes, called ''Iestyn Harris: There and Back – My Journey from League to Union and Back Again''.<ref>[https://www.amazon.co.uk/dp/1845960017 Iestyn Harris: There and Back – My Journey from League to Union and Back Again]</ref> Harris was part of the 2005 Bradford Bulls squad that became the first side in the history of Super League to win the Grand Final when finishing third in the final league ladder. He played for the Bradford Bulls at {{rlp|so}}, kicking a goal in their [[2005 Super League Grand Final]] victory against [[Leeds Rhinos]].

Harris was Bradford Bulls' captain for one year in 2006, taking over from Leeds-bound [[Jamie Peacock]]. As [[Super League]] champions Bradford Bulls faced [[National Rugby League]] premiers [[Wests Tigers]] in the [[2006 World Club Challenge]]. Harris captained the Bradford Bulls from {{rlp|so}}, kicking five goals from six attempts in their 30–10 victory. On 10 May 2006, Harris announced he would be retiring from playing international rugby league with [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]], just a week after being named in a 36-strong training squad for a June 2006 Test against New Zealand.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/4757641.stm Harris retires from Britain duty]</ref> Harris, who won the last of his 15 Great Britain caps against Australia in November 2005, said he wanted to concentrate on his club career. On 26 September 2006, he confirmed that he would continue to play for [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]], and was scheduled to return to the side against Scotland at the [[Brewery Field]], [[Bridgend]] in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]] qualifying match on Sunday 29 October 2006. He was forced to pull out through injury hours before kick-off. Harris kicked a club record 15 goals against [[Toulouse Olympique]] in a record 98–6 win on 19 April 2008. Harris had been linked to a [[player-coach]] role with [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]], but he decided to sign for [[RFL Championship|National League One]] club [[Featherstone Rovers]] instead.

===Featherstone Rovers===
Harris signed a 2-year deal at [[Featherstone Rovers]], as well as playing, he also took his first steps into coaching and working in the marketing department of the club, he made his debut for [[Featherstone Rovers]] on Sunday 15 February 2009, he also ultimately made a lot of debt for Bradford Bulls, and he played his last match for Featherstone Rovers during the 2009 season. On announcement of his tenure as assistant coach to [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]] at Celtic Crusaders, he agreed termination of his contract at Featherstone Rovers and an immediate end to his playing career.<ref name="BBCRet">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/celtic_crusaders/8307200.stm|title=Harris hangs up boots on career|publisher=BBC Sport|date=14 October 2009|access-date=14 October 2009}}</ref>

==Coaching career==

===Wales National Rugby League team===
While still playing for Bradford, Harris was appointed [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]] Assistant Coach on 18 June 2008. On 22 September 2009, Harris was appointed Wales's rugby league head coach, succeeding [[John Dixon (rugby league)|John Dixon]]. Appointed alongside Harris were the more experienced [[Kevin Ellis (rugby)|Kevin Ellis]] and [[Clive Griffiths (rugby)|Clive Griffiths]], and the trio's first match was against England on 17 October, which they lost 48–12.<ref name="BBC News - Sport - Harris' Challenge For Young Wales">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8269964.stm|title= Harris' Challenge For Young Wales|publisher=BBC|date=23 September 2009|access-date=24 September 2009}}</ref>

Wales qualified for the [[2011 Rugby League Four Nations]], and therefore won the Tournament's co-hosting rights with England. Unfortunately, as expected, Wales lost all of games, but they put up great fights. They lost by 42–4 against [[England national rugby league team|England]], but they played well against the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|Kiwis]] at [[Wembley Stadium]] in [[London]]. Wales lost by 36–0, but New Zealand were frustrated by not being able to play their attacking football full of flair, and this was due to Harris' team's good defence. Then surprisingly, in Wales' (and Welsh legend [[Lee Briers]] last game of his international career) final game of the Tournament, Wales shocked all odds, as they had an early 8–0 lead, against world heavyweights [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]], after 14 minutes. It took Australia until just before half-time to take the lead. Harris' men would then go on to lose the game 56–14, but he still gave Lee Briers a fitting farewell.

Iestyn coached Wales in the [[2013 Rugby League World Cup]] held in their own country and fellow British nation England. Unfortunately Iestyn's side didn't get a single win at the event, and they gave history to each of the teams that defeated them. [[Italy national rugby league team|Italy]] won their first World Cup match in their first appearance, [[United States national rugby league team|USA]] in their annual tournament advanced to the quarter-final, and the [[Cook Islands national rugby league team|Cook Islands]] won their first ever World Cup match.

On 9 April 2014, Harris resigned from the Wales post, after taking up the head coaching role at the [[Super League]] club, the [[Salford Red Devils]].<ref name="Iestyn Harris: Salford Red Devils coach steps down as Wales boss">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/0/rugby-league/26955090|title=Iestyn Harris: Salford Red Devils coach steps down as Wales boss|publisher=bbc.com|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

===Crusaders Rugby League===
On 14 October 2009, Harris was announced as assistant coach to [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]] at Wales-based [[Crusaders Rugby League]], alongside [[Jon Sharp]].<ref name="BBCRet"/> After a year as assistant it was announced in November 2010 that Harris would take over as head coach of [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]] following the resignation of [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]]. His first official game in charge was against [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] on Sunday 13 February 2011 in the opening round of the [[Super League]]. The game was played at the [[Millennium Stadium]] as part of the [[Magic Weekend]].

===Wigan Warriors===
In 2011, Harris became the assistant coach at former club Wigan.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.walesonline.co.uk/sport/rugby/rugby-news/rugby-league-crusaders-coach-iestyn-1817794|title=Crusaders coach Iestyn Harris set for Wigan role|date=24 August 2011|access-date=4 April 2014|publisher=Wales Online}}</ref>

===Salford Red Devils===
In 2014, it was announced by [[Marwan Koukash]] that Harris would take over from Brian Noble as head coach at [[Salford Red Devils]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/26893732|title=Iestyn Harris set to replace coach Brian Noble|date=4 April 2014|access-date=4 April 2014|publisher=BBC}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article/29909|title=Harris appointed Head Coach at Red Devils|date=4 April 2014|access-date=18 April 2014|publisher=Super League Official Website}}</ref> The appointment was described as a "huge gamble".<ref>{{cite news|last=Viller|first=Sam|url=http://www.loverugbyleague.com/blogpost_700-harris-appointment-is-koukash-s-biggest-gamble-yet.html|title=Harris appointment is Koukash's biggest gamble yet|date=9 April 2014|access-date=18 April 2014|publisher=Love Rugby League}}</ref> In Harris' first match in charge, Salford played away from home and defeated [[Bradford Bulls]] by 38–24.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,,11070_3686421,00.html|title=Iestyn Harris enjoys dream start as Salford win 38–24 at Bradford|date=11 April 2014|access-date=18 April 2014|publisher=Sky Sports}}</ref>

In September 2015, after being absent from the club for two months, Salford mutually sacked Harris from his post as head coach of the club.<ref>{{cite news
|title = Salford announce exit of head coach Iestyn Harris by mutual agreement
|publisher = theguradian
|date = 2 September 2015
|url = https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2015/sep/02/salford-departure-iestyn-harris-mutual-agreement
|access-date = 4 September 2015}}</ref>

==Media career==
Iestyn regularly provides expert TV and radio analysis including BBC's [[Super League Show]], and contributes to BBC Radio Five Live's rugby league coverage.{{citation needed|date=March 2023}}

==Author==
Harris released his autobiography ''Iestyn Harris: There and Back - My Journey from League to Union and Back Again'' in 2005.

==Personal life==
Harris lives in [[Todmorden|Cheshire]], England, he is married to Becky Harris with whom he has a daughter; Catrin, and a younger son; Cameron, he also has a sister; Rhiain.
